title: Unauthorized Physical Access Attempts at PSP

compliance.assurance_rationale: Uplifted from contributing to partial: UC provides meaningful monitoring evidence for CIP-006-6 R2.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NERC CIP CIP-006-6 R2 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.13.24: Unauthorized Physical Access Attempts at PSP.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.13.24' running on index=physical sourcetype=pacs:access, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

spl:
index=physical sourcetype=pacs:access earliest=-24h
    (result="denied" OR event_type IN ("forced","tailgate","passback"))
| lookup cip_psp_register reader_id OUTPUT psp_name bes_site
| stats count by event_type, psp_name, bes_site
| sort - count
description: We aggregate denied, forced, and tailgate physical access events for rapid triage and evidence of physical perimeter monitoring.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for NERC CIP compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes physical and sourcetypes pacs:access, pacs:access`. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Map readers to PSP inventory. (2) Push high severity to SOC bridge. (3) Optional join to VMS syslog. (4) Weekly trending for reliability. Indexes required: `index=physical`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=pacs:access`, `sourcetype=pacs:access``; Lookups: `cip_psp_register`.
visualization: Stacked bar (event types), Table (raw), Single value (tailgates). Key fields: `bes_site`, `event_type`, `psp_name`.
knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern — correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk OT Security Add-on (5151).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=physical` `sourcetype=pacs:access`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Map readers to PSP inventory. (2) Push high severity to SOC bridge. (3) Optional join to VMS syslog. (4) Weekly trending for reliability.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=physical sourcetype=pacs:access earliest=-24h
    (result="denied" OR event_type IN ("forced","tailgate","passback"))
| lookup cip_psp_register reader_id OUTPUT psp_name bes_site
| stats count by event_type, psp_name, bes_site
| sort - count
```

Understanding this SPL

**Unauthorized Physical Access Attempts at PSP (CIP-006-6 R2)** — We aggregate denied, forced, and tailgate physical access events for rapid triage and evidence of physical perimeter monitoring.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=physical` `sourcetype=pacs:access`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk OT Security Add-on (5151). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: physical; **sourcetype**: pacs:access. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=physical, sourcetype=pacs:access, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by event_type, psp_name, bes_site** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Stacked bar (event types), Table (raw), Single value (tailgates)
grandmaExplanation: We watch for we aggregate denied, forced, and tailgate physical access events for rapid triage and evidence of physical perimeter monitoring. This is critical because gaps here could mean serious regulatory exposure or security blind spots.
